title: CIL System — Full-length Mock 08
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.solution: Only II follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 30, A is 10th from left. B is 15th from right. C is exactly between A and B. Position of C from left:
questions.solution: 13th

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 6 km east, 8 km north, 6 km west, 2 km south. Direction from start:
questions.solution: North

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Mirror image of the number sequence '2025' when mirror is on left:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 'A @ B' means A is mother of B, 'A # B' means A is father of B, 'A $ B' means A is sister of B. In 'P @ Q $ R # S', how is P related to S?
questions.solution: Grandmother

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A clock gains 5 min per day. If set right at noon, what will it show at 6 AM the next day?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All papers are files. Some files are folders. All folders are covers. Conclusions: I. Some covers are files. II. Some folders are papers.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Six kids sit in circle. P is opposite Q. R is between P and S. T is left of Q. U is right of P. Who is between Q and U?
questions.solution: T

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: From point X, A is 40 m north. B is 30 m east of A. C is 40 m south of B. X is in which direction of C?
questions.solution: West

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these looks identical in a vertical mirror? H, O, X, N
questions.solution: H, O, X

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is B's mother's only daughter's son. How is A related to B's wife?
questions.solution: Son

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Angle between hour and minute hand at 6:20:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All chairs are tables. All tables are furniture. Some furniture is wood. Conclusions: I. Some chairs are wood. II. All chairs are furniture.
questions.solution: Only II follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Six friends sit in circle facing center. Rohan is between Mohan and Sohan. Tohan is opposite Rohan. Gohan is left of Tohan. Johan is right of Mohan. Who is between Sohan and Tohan?
questions.solution: Johan

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A dice numbered 1-6. If 1 is at top and 2 at front, which is at bottom if standard dice?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Silence : Noise :: Stillness : ?
questions.solution: Movement

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the missing: AD, GJ, MP, ?
questions.solution: SV

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Bar graph: Incomes of A=40000, B=50000, C=35000, D=45000. Ratio of C to average:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In code, MINING = 84, COAL = 38, GEOLOGY = ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: Z, Y, W, T, P, ?
questions.solution: K

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A, B, C, D, E sit in a line. A is not at ends. B is right of A but not at end. C is left of D. E is at left end. Who is in middle?
questions.solution: B

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 'P + Q' means P is father of Q, 'P - Q' means P is mother of Q, 'P × Q' means P is brother of Q, 'P ÷ Q' means P is sister of Q, then in 'A ÷ B + C - D', how is A related to D?
questions.solution: Grandmother

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: is / of / coal / formation / a / process / slow
options: Coal formation is a slow process
A slow process is formation of coal
Formation of coal is a slow process
Coal formation is a slow process
Coal is a slow process of formation
questions.solution: Coal formation is a slow process

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of CATALYST:
questions.solution: Trigger

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: innovation / for / key / is / growth / economic
options: Innovation is key for economic growth
Innovation is key for economic growth
Economic growth is key for innovation
For economic growth innovation is key
Key for growth economic is innovation
questions.solution: Innovation is key for economic growth

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of MAGNANIMOUS:
questions.solution: Generous

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: project / the / completed / was / time / on
options: The project was completed on time
Was the project completed on time
Completed was the project on time
The project was completed on time
On time the project was completed
questions.solution: The project was completed on time

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of SPURIOUS:
questions.solution: Fake

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: digital / has / way / the / changed / communication / we
options: Communication digital has changed the way we
We communicate way the has changed digital technology
Digital technology has changed the way we communicate
The way we communicate has changed digital technology
Digital technology has changed the way we communicate
questions.solution: Digital technology has changed the way we communicate

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of ARDENT:
questions.solution: Enthusiastic

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: automation / loss / leads / to / jobs / of
options: Leads to loss of jobs automation
Loss of jobs automation leads to
Automation leads to loss of jobs
Jobs loss of to leads automation
Automation leads to loss of jobs
questions.solution: Automation leads to loss of jobs

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of ABUNDANT:
questions.solution: Plentiful

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: sustainable / practices / mining / promote / must
options: Sustainable development must promote mining practices
Mining practices must promote sustainable development
Must promote sustainable development mining practices
Practices mining sustainable must promote development
Mining practices must promote sustainable development
questions.solution: Mining practices must promote sustainable development

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of RESILIENT:
questions.solution: Tough

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: globalisation / economies / has / world / around / the / transformed
options: Around the world globalisation has transformed economies
Globalisation has transformed economies around the world
Globalisation has transformed economies around the world
Transformed economies globalisation has around the world
Economies around the world has globalisation transformed
questions.solution: Globalisation has transformed economies around the world

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of LACONIC:
questions.solution: Brief

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: plays / role / coal / in / energy / a / vital / production / India
options: Coal plays a vital role in India energy production
Vital role coal plays in energy production India
In India coal plays a energy vital production role
Coal in India plays vital role energy production
Coal plays a vital role in India energy production
questions.solution: Coal plays a vital role in India energy production

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of BENEVOLENT:
questions.solution: Charitable

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: safety / should / mines / our / be / in / priority / top
options: Safety in mines should be our top priority
In mines safety our top priority should be
Top priority safety should be in mines
Safety should be our top priority in mines
Safety should be our top priority in mines
questions.solution: Safety should be our top priority in mines

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The government ____ the emergency protocol yesterday.
questions.solution: activated

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: He prefers coffee ____ tea in the morning.
questions.solution: to

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: government / measures / safety / implemented / has / stringent
options: Stringent safety measures government has implemented
Government has implemented stringent safety measures
Safety measures stringent implemented has government
Implemented has government stringent safety measures
Government has implemented stringent safety measures
questions.solution: Government has implemented stringent safety measures

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of AMICABLE:
questions.solution: Hostile

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To make no bones about':
questions.solution: To speak frankly

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of OBSTINATE:
questions.solution: Flexible

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To be caught between two stools':
questions.solution: To be unable to choose

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of JEOPARDIZE:
questions.solution: Protect

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The language of palindromes over {a, b} is:
questions.solution: Palindrome = ww^R. CFG: S → aSa | bSb | a | b | ε. Not regular.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer in C stores:
options: The memory address of a variable
questions.solution: Pointer = variable holding address of another variable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 2 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 2KB = 2^11 bytes, so the offset field is 11 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory to physical translation uses:
questions.solution: Virtual address → page table → physical frame. TLB caches recent translations.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: System F (polymorphic lambda calculus):
options: Supports parametric polymorphism (type variables, universal types)
questions.solution: System F: ΛX.λx:X.x (identity function with type parameter). Can define Church numerals, pairs, etc.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A function that calls itself is called:
questions.solution: Recursion = function calls itself (directly or indirectly).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Symbolic links:
options: Can only link files in same directory
Store the path to the target file (separate inode, can cross filesystems)
questions.solution: Symlink: ln -s target link_name. Dangling if target deleted. Separate inode with target path.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dark silicon:
options: Only a fraction of a chip can run at full power simultaneously (due to power/thermal limits)
questions.solution: Dark Silicon: not all transistors can be powered simultaneously. Leads to specialized accelerators (DSP, GPU, ASIC).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Halting problem for Turing machines is:
options: Undecidable (no algorithm can solve it for all inputs)
questions.solution: Halting problem (Turing, 1936): undecidable. No program can determine if another program halts.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Function overloading in C++:
options: Same name different return types only
Multiple functions with the same name but different parameters
questions.solution: Overloading: name mangling differentiates overloads (must differ in parameter types/count).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The IEEE 754 single-precision float has:
options: 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 23 mantissa bits (32 bits total)
questions.solution: IEEE 754: single (32-bit): biases exponent by 127. Double (64-bit): 1+11+52, bias 1023.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Ackermann function:
options: Is computable but not primitive recursive (grows extremely fast)
questions.solution: A(0,n)=n+1, A(m+1,0)=A(m,1), A(m+1,n+1)=A(m,A(m+1,n)). Not primitive recursive.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The difference between struct and union in C:
options: struct members have separate memory; union members share the same memory
union members are all accessible simultaneously
questions.solution: Union: size = max member size. Members overlay each other (only one active at a time).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The page fault frequency is reduced by:
options: Increasing the number of frames allocated to a process
questions.solution: Working set model: allocate enough frames to cover working set to reduce faults.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Turing machine with multiple tapes is:
options: Equivalent in power to a single-tape TM
questions.solution: Multi-tape TM can be simulated by single-tape TM (quadratic slowdown).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'self' parameter in Python methods refers to:
questions.solution: 'self' = current instance (like 'this' in C++/Java). First param of instance methods.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'kill' command in Unix sends:
questions.solution: kill -9 = SIGKILL (forced termination). kill -15 = SIGTERM (graceful).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 48 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 48 bits ÷ 8 = 6 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Arden's lemma helps to:
options: Solve regular expression equations (R = Q + RP → R = QP*)
questions.solution: Arden's lemma: equation R = Q + RP has unique solution R = QP* (provided ε ∉ P).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inheritance in OOP allows:
options: A class to derive properties and methods from another class
questions.solution: Inheritance = IS-A relationship (reuse + extension).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 200 ns, page-fault rate = 0.001, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×200 + 0.001×10ms ≈ 10199.8 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The instruction cycle consists of:
questions.solution: Instruction cycle: fetch → decode → execute → (write back).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Beta reduction in lambda calculus:
questions.solution: β-reduction: the computational step of lambda calculus. Normal form = no more β-redexes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An abstract class in C++ has:
options: At least one pure virtual function
questions.solution: Abstract class = class with = 0 pure virtual function (cannot instantiate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The virtual memory size is limited by:
questions.solution: Virtual address space = 2^n where n = address bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Type-0 grammar corresponds to:
questions.solution: Type 0 = unrestricted grammar = TM (most powerful).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism allows:
options: Only one form of a method
The same method name to behave differently on different objects
questions.solution: Polymorphism = many forms (compile-time: overloading; runtime: overriding).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 1 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 1KB = 2^10 bytes, so the offset field is 10 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data hazard occurs when:
options: An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline
Two instructions use the same hardware
questions.solution: Data hazard = read-after-write (RAW) dependency.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A context-free language is recognized by:
questions.solution: CFL ↔ PDA. Regular ↔ DFA/NFA. CSL ↔ LBA. RE ↔ TM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory alignment requirements:
options: All types can be stored anywhere
Certain types must be stored at addresses that are multiples of their size
questions.solution: Natural alignment: int at multiple of 4, pointer at multiple of 8 (64-bit). Misaligned access = slower or crash.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compared with a process, a thread:
options: Shares the address space of its process
Has its own copy of global data
Has a completely separate address space
questions.solution: Threads of a process share code, data and heap, but have their own stack and registers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: False sharing in multiprocessors:
options: True sharing of the same variable
Different processors modify different variables on the same cache line (unnecessary invalidations)
questions.solution: False sharing: thrashing due to cache coherence protocol (even though variables are independent).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A context-free grammar consists of:
options: V, T, P, S (variables, terminals, productions, start)
questions.solution: CFG = (V, T, P, S) where V = non-terminals, T = terminals, P = rules, S = start.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Padding in C structs:
options: The compiler adds unused bytes between members to satisfy alignment requirements
questions.solution: Struct padding: members aligned to their natural boundary. Total size may exceed sum of sizes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Deadlock can be prevented by:
options: Ensuring at least one of the 4 Coffman conditions doesn't hold
questions.solution: Deadlock prevention: violate one of mutex, hold-and-wait, no-preemption, or circular wait.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 16K memory locations?
questions.solution: 16K = 16384 = 2^14, so 14 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The pumping lemma for CFL is used to prove:
questions.solution: Pumping lemma: if L is CFL, strings ≥ p can be pumped. Contrapositive: can't pump → not CFL.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The output of printf('%d', 5/2) in C is:
questions.solution: Integer division: 5/2 = 2 in C (truncates). For 2.5, use 5/2.0.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Priority inversion occurs when:
options: A high-priority process runs too long
A low-priority process holds a lock needed by a high-priority process
questions.solution: Priority inversion solved by priority inheritance protocol.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MESI cache coherence protocol:
options: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid (four states per cache line)
questions.solution: MESI: M=modified (dirty, exclusive), E=clean exclusive, S=shared (clean), I=invalid.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CNF (Chomsky Normal Form) requires productions to be:
options: A → BC or A → a (or S → ε)
questions.solution: CNF: rules of length 2 (A→BC) or terminal (A→a). Useful for CYK algorithm.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Variadic templates in C++:
options: Accept a fixed number of arguments
Templates that accept a variable number of arguments (parameter pack)
questions.solution: Variadic: template<typename... Args> void f(Args... args). sizeof...(Args) for count.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 500 MB at 50 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 500/50 = 10 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bi-endian processors:
options: Can be configured for either big-endian or little-endian mode
questions.solution: Bi-endian: ARM, PowerPC, MIPS. Endianness set at boot or per page.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Halting problem asks:
options: Does a given TM halt on a given input? (undecidable)
Does a TM accept a string?
questions.solution: Halting problem (Turing 1936): no TM can decide if an arbitrary program halts.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: constexpr in C++:
options: Evaluates expressions at compile time (enables compile-time computation)
questions.solution: constexpr: function or variable evaluated at compile time. constexpr functions can be called at runtime too.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Hard links share:
options: The same inode (same file data, different directory entries)
questions.solution: Hard link: ln target link_name. Both point to same inode. Can't cross filesystems or link directories.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 1K memory locations?
questions.solution: 1K = 1024 = 2^10, so 10 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Cook-Levin theorem shows SAT is:
questions.solution: Cook-Levin (1971): SAT is NP-complete (every NP problem reduces to SAT).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operator overloading in C++:
options: Customizes the behavior of operators for user-defined types
questions.solution: Operator overloading: must have at least one user-defined type operand. E.g., operator+ for Matrix.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'df' command shows:
questions.solution: df -h: human-readable. df -i: inode usage. du: directory usage.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Little-endian byte order stores:
options: Least significant byte first (at lowest address)
Bytes in reverse order of address
questions.solution: Little-endian: LSB at lowest address (x86). Big-endian: MSB at lowest address (network order).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Church numerals represent:
questions.solution: Church: 0 = λf.λx.x, 1 = λf.λx.f x, 2 = λf.λx.f(f x). Successor = λn.λf.λx.f(n f x).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The difference between call by value and call by reference is:
options: Call by value copies; call by reference passes the address
questions.solution: Call by value: parameter is a copy. Call by reference: parameter is a reference (alias).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The three-way handshake in TCP establishes:
questions.solution: TCP handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The state minimization of a DFA uses:
options: Minimization by removing unreachable states only
questions.solution: DFA minimization: partition refinement (equivalent states merged).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=2 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 7.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A D flip-flop stores:
questions.solution: D flip-flop = 1-bit memory element (data latch).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Concurrency control using optimistic locking:
options: Assumes conflicts are rare; checks at commit time (version/timestamp)
questions.solution: Optimistic locking: read version, write only if version unchanged. Better for low contention.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP window scale option:
options: Allows windows larger than 64 KB (scaling factor)
questions.solution: Window scaling: multiplies window field by 2^scale_factor (RFC 1323).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The bandwidth of a network is measured in:
questions.solution: Bandwidth = data rate (bits/second). Hertz = frequency.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: AVX-512 registers are:
questions.solution: AVX-512: 32 × 512-bit registers. 8 mask registers (k0-k7). Opmask-based predication.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: 1NF requires:
questions.solution: 1NF = atomic columns (no arrays or nested tables).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The result of subtracting 0110 from 1010 using 2's complement is:
questions.solution: Take 2's complement of 0110 = 1010. Add to 1010: 1010+1010=0100 (ignore carry).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 11, push 12, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: NFV (Network Function Virtualization):
options: Runs network functions (firewall, NAT, load balancer) as virtualized software
questions.solution: NFV: virtualized network services on commodity hardware. VNF = Virtual Network Function.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: HTTP runs on which port?
questions.solution: HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, SMTP = 25, SSH = 22.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cookie in HTTP:
options: Small data stored by browser, sent with each request to the server
questions.solution: Cookies: session management, personalization, tracking. Set-Cookie header → Cookie header.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A firewall can:
options: Filter packets based on IP, port, protocol (stateful or stateless)
questions.solution: Firewall: packet filter, stateful inspection, application-layer (proxy), next-gen.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: HTTP 2.0 features include:
questions.solution: HTTP/2: binary framing, multiplexed streams, header compression (HPACK), server push.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 1 Mbps has a round-trip time of 100 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 1Mbps×100ms ÷ 8 ≈ 12.21 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DHCP assigns:
options: IP addresses dynamically to devices on a network
questions.solution: DHCP: IP + subnet mask + gateway + DNS via DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Ack).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of edges in a complete graph with n nodes is:
questions.solution: K_n has n(n-1)/2 edges.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: IPv4 address size is:
questions.solution: IPv4 = 32 bits. IPv6 = 128 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of binary search for n=256 is:
questions.solution: Binary search halves the search space each step: O(log n) ≈ O(log 256).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=8 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 511.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'copy-on-write' technique is used during:
options: Fork() system call to share pages until modified
questions.solution: COW: forked child shares parent pages until one writes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bucket sort works best when:
options: Input is uniformly distributed across buckets
questions.solution: Bucket sort: O(n + k) average when uniform distribution. Worst O(n²) when all in 1 bucket.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Accessing the i-th element of an array by index takes time:
questions.solution: Arrays support constant-time random access via base address + index×size.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Race-around condition occurs in:
options: JK flip-flop (when J=K=1 and clock pulse too long)
questions.solution: Race-around: JK with J=K=1 oscillates if clock pulse > propagation delay. Solved by master-slave.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number FF₁₆ is:
questions.solution: FF₁₆ = 255₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The relational model was proposed by:
questions.solution: Codd's 1970 paper 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks'.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Nagle's algorithm:
options: Bundles small outgoing TCP segments to reduce overhead
questions.solution: Nagle: delay sending if there's unACKed data and data < MSS (improves efficiency).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 11, push 20, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The leftist heap:
options: Supports merge in O(log n) (null path length property, left subtree heavier)
questions.solution: Leftist heap: dist(null) = 0, dist(node) = 1 + min(dist(left), dist(right)). Left dist ≥ right dist.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm is used for deadlock:
questions.solution: It checks whether granting a request leaves the system in a safe state (avoidance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=16 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 131071.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 11, push 12, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">11</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">3</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An enum in C:
options: Defines a set of named integer constants
questions.solution: enum: values default to 0, 1, 2... Can assign custom values. Type-safe in C++.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The edit distance problem measures:
options: Minimum operations (insert, delete, substitute) to convert one string to another
questions.solution: Edit distance = DP for string transformation cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Subtraction using 2's complement: 1010 - 0110 = :

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Atomicity ensures:
options: A transaction is fully completed or fully rolled back (no partial updates)
questions.solution: Atomicity: if any part fails, all changes are undone (rollback).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Microprogramming is:
options: Implementing the control unit using micro-instructions stored in control memory
questions.solution: Microprogrammed control: ROM stores microcode for each instruction (easier to design).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'aging' technique prevents:
options: Starvation by gradually increasing priority of waiting processes
questions.solution: Aging: priority of waiting process increases over time → eventually chosen.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 6 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 6×5/2 = 15 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The MVC pattern separates:
questions.solution: MVC: Model manages data, View displays, Controller handles input.
